human_use.rationale: The Artifact is a human-operated mobile checkout. Its success depends on discoverability, navigation, input burden, error recovery, feedback, accessibility, touch ergonomics, interruption recovery, perceived latency, destructive-action safety, and cognitive load across cross-disability use.

objective_reports.treatment: accepted; qualitative scoring does not override objective gates

score_calculation: Arithmetic mean of 11 integer lens scores: 39/11

discoverability.finding: Numbered card headings, Complete/Incomplete states, and always-present review identify the work, though four open cards dilute the first task.

navigation.finding: Keyboard-operated edit buttons moved focus to Contact, Address, Shipping, and Payment; all sections remained directly reachable.

input_burden.finding: The required data are not duplicated, but all inputs are exposed in one long mobile traversal.

error_prevention_recovery.finding: Empty submission produced a focused visible summary and retained filled values; linked correction path was operable.

feedback_status.finding: Visual card completion state is useful, but feedback is less prominent between task changes than in the wizard.

accessibility.finding: Keyboard shipping and review edits worked; accepted objective report passes, with visible focus and errors evident in screenshots.

responsive_touch_ergonomics.finding: No horizontal overflow at 320/360/390, but dense cards, compact radio rows, and very long scroll reduce mobile comfort.

interruption_resumption.finding: All safe values restored and card number/expiry/security code cleared after reload.

latency_perception.finding: Processing feedback completes safely, but the visual busy treatment is modest.

destructive_actions.finding: Review confirmation and duplicate-safe order placement reduce accidental finalisation; repeated activation yielded one confirmation.

cognitive_load.finding: Clear card grouping helps, but simultaneous exposure of every task and heavy outlines create scanning load.

visual_information_clarity.finding: Information is readable but the dense all-open cards and heavy outlines make hierarchy visually louder than necessary.

strengths: All task cards, review edits, keyboard shipping selection, safe reload, reduced motion, and confirmation were operable with no console or runtime errors.
Focused error summary and persistent Complete/Incomplete card states make recovery and work status visible.
defects: All four task cards are open at once on mobile, creating a long, visually repetitive form before review.
The high-contrast, heavy double-outline treatment makes cards and primary action visually noisy rather than prioritising content.
uncertainty: Screen-reader speech output and real switch-scanning dwell burden were not available; semantic objective results are accepted as passing.
loop_02_recommendation: Preserve independently editable cards, but collapse completed card details or add a concise review/status rail so mobile users do not repeatedly traverse the full form.
